title: A Roboticist-Turned-Teacher and His Students Built a Life-Size ENIAC Replica
summary:
In a classroom in Gilbert, Arizona, a group of middle and high school students are putting the finishing touches on a massive machine: a full-scale replica of ENIAC, one of the world's first programmable electronic computers. The original ENIAC was built in 1946 and was about 1,000 times faster than any other computing machine of its time. It filled an entire room, with 40 panels arranged in a U-shape.

The project is the brainchild of Tom Burick, a technology instructor at PS Academy, a school for students with autism and other specialized learning needs. Burick is no ordinary teacher. Before entering the classroom, he ran White Box Robotics, a company that developed a modular robot platform called the 914 PC-Bot. "The robot's chassis was like a box of Legos," Burick explains. "You could click together two torsos to double its payload, switch out the drive system, or swap its head for a different set of sensors." His company sold about 200 robots to 17 countries before the 2008 financial crisis forced it to close in 2010.

Burick's path to teaching was shaped by his own experiences as a neurodivergent learner. He has dyscalculia, a condition that makes traditional mathematics challenging. But he reframes it as a strength. "I have this CAD program that runs in my head 24 hours a day," he says. "I think the reason I was successful in robotics, truly, was because of the dyscalculia. To me, it has always been a superpower."

As a teenager in Latrobe, Pennsylvania, Burick built a 150-pound firefighting robot that won awards from IEEE. He reached out to mentors at Carnegie Mellon University and Saint Vincent College, who gave him textbooks and let him sit in on classes. "They really changed the trajectory of my life," he says. "I thought I need to pay that forward."

Now, through the ENIAC replica project, Burick is doing just that. The project allows his students — many of whom share traits like the ability to hyperfocus and precisely repeat tasks — to apply their strengths to a real-world historical challenge. "I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour," Burick says. Instead, they're building a piece of computing history, one panel at a time.
why_it_matters: This story challenges stereotypes about neurodivergence by showing how conditions like autism and dyscalculia can be assets in hands-on engineering. It also demonstrates the power of mentorship and how real-world projects can inspire students who might otherwise be overlooked.

keywords.explanation: One of the first programmable electronic computers, built in 1946

A school in Arizona for students with autism and other learning needs
The robotics company Tom Burick ran before teaching
A modular robot platform Burick developed that clicked together like Legos
A learning disability affecting math, which Burick considers a superpower

It makes him bad at teaching

background_read: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was built during World War II to calculate artillery firing tables. It used 18,000 vacuum tubes, weighed 30 tons, and consumed enough electricity to power a small town.
Vacuum tubes are glass tubes that control electrical current; they were the key component of early computers before transistors were invented.
The replica project at PS Academy is not just about history – it gives neurodivergent students a hands-on way to apply their unique cognitive strengths, such as hyperfocus and pattern recognition.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Students in Arizona are putting finishing touches on a life-size replica of ENIAC.
└── SPECIFIC: 'The robot's chassis was like a box of Legos' – Burick on his 914 PC-Bot.
KEY FIGURE: Tom Burick – former robotics entrepreneur, now teacher.
├── BACKGROUND: Ran White Box Robotics, sold ~200 robots to 17 countries.
├── NEURODIVERSITY: Has dyscalculia, which he calls a superpower.
└── MOTIVATION: Mentors from Carnegie Mellon and St. Vincent changed his life; he wants to pay it forward.
├── GOAL: Create a real-world challenge for students with autism/hyperfocus.
└── QUOTE: 'I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour.'
IMPACT: Students apply strengths like precision and tenacity.
OPEN QUESTION: What future projects will Burick and his students tackle next?

perspectives.description: Students gain confidence, practical skills, and a sense of achievement. The project validates their unique abilities and shows they can succeed in STEAM fields.
Such projects require significant funding, space, and teacher dedication. Many schools lack the resources to replicate this model, limiting its scalability.
Recreating ENIAC is a valuable educational tool, but it's a replica, not an exact working model. The focus is more on the process and inspiration than perfect historical fidelity.

If successful, this approach could inspire other schools to create hands-on history projects that accommodate diverse learners, potentially increasing neurodivergent representation in technology careers.
